docs.title: UiPath Agentic Practice Workshop
docs.text:
Learner's Handbook — a practical guide for hands-on exercises

Training Environment

  • Platform: cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs
  • Tenant: AgenticPractice
  • Contact: sergey.shumilov@uipath.com

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text: This workshop guides you through building real agentic automation solutions on the UiPath Platform. You'll work through two main use cases, each progressively adding intelligence and orchestration complexity.

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Focused, domain-specific AI solutions consistently outperform general-purpose chatbots.

The exercises apply software engineering discipline — structured process design, systematic testing, and incremental iteration — to AI automation projects.

docs.title: 1. Categorizing Incidents
docs.text:
Build an intelligent ServiceNow incident triage agent, progressing through five phases:

Phase Focus Basic agent Foundational categorization using system prompts LLM with Context Ground the agent in real incident data to eliminate hallucinations Evaluations Test performance across standardized test cases Live Integration Connect to ServiceNow API — read and write real incidents Tools & Escalations Route complex cases to humans via Action Center

Start Exercise →

docs.title: 2. Invoice Matching Automation
docs.text:
Build a multi-component process combining AI agents, robots, human validation, and API integrations using Maestro orchestration.

Start Exercise →

docs.title: 3. Invoice Matching with IXP
docs.text:
Extend the invoice matching workflow with Intelligent eXtraction & Processing for automated data extraction from PDF invoices with confidence-based escalation.

Start Exercise →

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
Agent Builder — Low-code environment for creating, designing, and deploying agents.

Maestro — UiPath's orchestration platform for coordinating humans, robots, and AI agents across long-running enterprise processes.

IXP (Intelligent eXtraction & Processing) — Document processing technology with confidence scoring for automated data extraction from unstructured documents.

Action Center — UiPath interface for human escalation workflows, exception handling, and manual review.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: You've built a complete agentic orchestration. Here's what to do next.

docs.title: What You Accomplished
docs.text:
By completing these exercises, you built a production-pattern automation that combines:

Component Role Robots Structured, deterministic task execution AI Agents Intelligent decision-making and data processing Humans Judgment, validation, and exception handling APIs System integration and data exchange

docs.title: 1. Publish and Run Your Process
docs.text:
Publish your completed process to your personal workspace, then run it as you would any other UiPath process.

  • Open Maestro in your workspace
  • Publish the process
  • Trigger a test run with sample data
  • Review the execution trace

docs.title: 2. Explore Maestro Analytics
docs.text:
After a few runs, Maestro analytics become available:

  • Transaction-level execution details
  • End-to-end process flow visualization
  • Bottleneck identification
  • Exception and escalation rates

Open Maestro → Analytics to explore your process performance.

docs.title: Keep Iterating
docs.text:
The real learning happens in experimentation:

Prompt engineering - Modify your agent's system prompt - Test how changes affect categorization or matching decisions - Try different approaches for handling edge cases

Test the boundaries - Feed your agent unusual or ambiguous inputs - See where it fails or produces unexpected results - Design for those failure modes

Expand the process - Add a new document type to the matching process - Introduce a new escalation path - Connect to an additional system

docs.title: Learn More
docs.text:
Resource Description UiPath Academy Official learning paths for Agent Builder and Maestro UiPath Documentation API references, platform guides, release notes UiPath Community Forum for questions, shared automations, and tips

Questions?

Contact: sergey.shumilov@uipath.com

docs.title: Categorizing Incidents
docs.text: Build an intelligent ServiceNow incident triage agent

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: In this exercise you'll use UiPath Agent Builder to create an agent that automatically categorizes and routes ServiceNow incidents. You'll progress through five phases, each adding a new capability layer.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Basic Agent
docs.text:
Create a foundational categorization agent using system prompts. The agent analyzes incident descriptions and assigns categories without any external data sources.

You'll learn: - Agent Builder basics - Writing effective system prompts for categorization - Testing agent responses

docs.title: Phase 2 — LLM with Context
docs.text:
Ground your agent in real incident data to eliminate hallucinations. Instead of relying solely on the LLM's general knowledge, you'll provide structured context from your incident database.

You'll learn: - Data-anchoring techniques - Preventing AI-generated assumptions - Feeding structured data into prompts

docs.title: Phase 3 — Evaluations
docs.text:
Deploy an evaluation framework to systematically test your agent's performance across a standardized collection of test cases.

You'll learn: - Building test case collections - Running batch evaluations - Measuring and improving categorization accuracy

docs.title: Phase 4 — Live System Integration
docs.text:
Connect the agent to ServiceNow's live API — retrieve incident details from real systems and post categorization decisions back.

You'll learn: - API integration in Agent Builder - Reading from and writing to ServiceNow - Working with real operational data

docs.title: Phase 5 — Tools and Escalations
docs.text:
Implement escalation workflows via Action Center. Route complex or ambiguous incidents to human reviewers when automated handling isn't appropriate.

You'll learn: - Configuring Action Center escalations - Designing human-in-the-loop decision points - When (and how) to hand off to humans

docs.title: Platform
docs.text:
All exercises run in UiPath Agent Builder, a low-code environment that lets you create, design, and deploy agents without deep coding experience.

Training Environment

Log in at cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs using tenant AgenticPractice.

docs.title: LLM with Context
docs.text: Build a context-grounded ServiceNow incident categorization agent

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Create a ServiceNow incident categorization agent in Agent Builder, configure its prompts, ground it in real data using Context Grounding, and validate its performance with Evaluations.

docs.title: How Context Grounding Works
docs.text: Without grounding, an LLM may produce plausible-sounding but incorrect category–subcategory pairs — combinations that don't exist in your system. Context Grounding anchors the agent to a real data source, so every decision traces back to valid entries in your context.

docs.title: What the Evaluations Step Adds
docs.text: Evaluations let you run a batch of test cases against your agent before deploying it. You define expected outputs, run the set, and measure how well the agent performs across edge cases.

docs.title: Part 1: Create and configure the agent
docs.text:
1. In Studio Web, create a new Agent Solution.

  2. Rename the solution to ServiceNow Incidents Management Solution.

  3. Name the agent ServiceNow Incidents Management Agent.

  4. Open the Data Manager panel and add the following Input Arguments (type: String):

    Argument Description IncidentShortDescription Short description of the ServiceNow incident IncidentDescription Full description of the ServiceNow incident

  5. Add the following Output Arguments:

    Argument Description IncidentCategory Category assigned to the incident IncidentSubcategory Subcategory assigned to the incident AssigneeEmail Email address of the assigned expert ExecutionDetails Summary of the classification steps taken

docs.title: Part 2: Configure the agent prompts
docs.text:
The agent uses two prompts: a System Prompt that defines its role and rules, and a User Prompt that structures how your inputs are passed in.

  1. Enter the following text in the System Prompt field:

    You are a ServiceNow Incidents categorization agent. Analyze incident details and determine the correct Category, Subcategory, and Assignee email address.

  2. Enter the following text in the User Prompt field:

    Analyze and categorize the following ServiceNow incident:
Incident Short Description: {{IncidentShortDescription}}
Incident Description: {{IncidentDescription}}

  3. Test the agent with these sample values:

    • Short Description: CRM software crashes on launch
    • Description: Every time I try to open the CRM software, it crashes immediately after the splash screen. This has been happening for the past week and is affecting my ability to manage customer interactions.

docs.title: Part 3: Add Context Grounding
docs.text:
1. Enable Context Grounding in the agent configuration.

  2. Select ServiceNow Incidents Categorization Information as the context source.

    The context contains only valid Category–Subcategory pairs. The agent must select from this list — it must not generate combinations that are not present.

  3. Import the SNOW Assignee Lookup Automation project.

  4. Add the imported automation as a tool. Use this description:

    Use this tool to determine the email address of the on-duty expert for a given incident category and subcategory.

docs.title: Part 4: Test with Evaluations
docs.text:
1. Go to the Evaluation Sets tab.

  2. Import the provided JSON evaluation set. It contains 8 test cases:

    # Short Description Expected Category 1 Database connection issue Database_MSSQL 2 Email sending failure Software_Email 3 Laptop slowdown Inquiry_Antivirus 4 CRM software crash Inquiry_ExternalApplication 5 Display flicker Hardware_Monitor 6 Website access issue Network_DNS 7 VPN disconnection Network_VPN 8 Windows update failure Software_OS

  3. Run the evaluation set and review the results.

← Back to Overview | Next: Tools and Escalations →

docs.title: Tools and Escalations
docs.text: Connect your agent to ServiceNow and route ambiguous cases to humans

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add two ServiceNow tools to your agent so it can retrieve and update live incident data. Then configure an escalation path that routes cases the agent cannot confidently categorize to a human reviewer via Action Center.

docs.title: How Tools Work
docs.text: Tools extend what your agent can do. Instead of only analyzing text, the agent can now call external systems — querying ServiceNow for incident data and writing the categorization result back. Each tool has a description that tells the agent when and how to use it.

docs.title: When to Escalate
docs.text: Escalation is not a failure — it is a design choice. Use it whenever the agent cannot establish a clear category and subcategory from the incident description. A well-configured escalation path is more valuable than an agent that guesses.

docs.title: Part 1: Add ServiceNow tools
docs.text:
1. Open your agent in Agent Builder and go to the Tools tab.

  2. Add the Search Incidents by Incident Number tool from the ServiceNow catalog.

  3. Select the shared ServiceNow connection from the ServiceNow Incidents folder.

  4. Add the UpdateServiceNowIncident tool. Configure its argument descriptions exactly as follows:

    Argument Description Assignee Assignee email address Incident ID The ID of the ServiceNow incident — do not use the Incident Number Category The Category of the ServiceNow incident Subcategory The Subcategory of the ServiceNow incident

    Tip

    A ServiceNow incident has two identifiers: ID (a unique string like 36155...53afb2) and Number (a human-readable label like INC0111888). The update tool requires the ID, not the Number.

  5. Update the agent's input arguments. Remove all existing input arguments and add a single one:

    Argument Type Description IncidentNumber String The Number of the ServiceNow incident to categorize

  6. Update the User Prompt to include the incident number:

    Analyze and categorize the following ServiceNow incident:
Incident Short Description: {{IncidentShortDescription}}
Incident Description: {{IncidentDescription}}
Incident Number: {{IncidentNumber}}
Determine the appropriate category, subcategory, and assignee email for this incident based on the provided information.

  7. Update the System Prompt to instruct the agent how to use the tools:

    Determine the Incident Category and Subcategory based on Description and Short Description from Categorization Information Context. Context contains table with only possible Category-Subcategory pairs. Do not mix Category-Subcategory pairs if specific pair is not present in the context. Pick the Category-Subcategory pair that aligns well with Incident Descriptions. If you are not sure or no category pair is a clear match, use escalation.

    Use the Search Incidents tool with IncidentNumber as input to retrieve incident details. Stop if an assignee already exists. After categorizing, determine the on-duty assignee using the Assignee Lookup automation. Update the ticket using the UpdateServiceNowIncident tool. Take no action if category, subcategory, or assignee cannot be established.

  8. Test the updated agent using the Ticket Management App with a sample incident number. Categorized incidents move to the bottom list after processing.

docs.title: Part 2: Add escalations
docs.text:
1. Add an escalation path using the ServiceNow Agent Escalation App from the ServiceNow Incidents folder.

  2. Configure the escalation tool with this prompt:

    Use this when you cannot establish category and subcategory of the Incident based on Description and Short Description.

  3. Add the in_Reasoning argument with this description:

    Brief explanation of the steps taken before escalating

    Configure the escalation outcomes: - Submit → Continue execution - Stop → End execution

  4. Update the System Prompt to add the escalation handling instructions:

    If Category and Subcategory have been selected by the user as part of escalation, look up Assignee based on selected Category and Subcategory, and then update ticket. Only use email addresses retrieved from the lookup tool, do not generate email addresses.

  5. Test the escalation path with this sample incident:

    • Short Description: I would like to talk with a manager
    • Description: Every time I reach out to this team, the response time is really long, seriously affecting my productivity. I would like to talk with a manager please.

    The agent should recognize it cannot categorize this incident and trigger the escalation, creating a task in Action Center for a human reviewer.

Your agent is now complete. It retrieves incident details from ServiceNow, categorizes them using grounded context, updates the ticket, and escalates when it cannot determine a clear category.

← Back to Overview

docs.title: Invoice Matching Automation
docs.text: 2-Way matching with standard orchestration

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: In this exercise you'll build a comprehensive multi-component automation using Maestro to orchestrate AI agents, robots, human validators, and external APIs into a single end-to-end invoice matching process.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text:
Agentic orchestration, implemented in Maestro, empowers enterprise customers to coordinate their long-running enterprise processes between humans, robots, and AI agents, working seamlessly across different systems of records and engagement.

Maestro lets you design, implement, monitor, operate, and optimize long-running processes at scale by combining three participant types:

Participant Role Robot Structured, rule-based task execution AI Agent Intelligent decision-making Human Judgment, validation, exceptions

docs.title: Five-Step Implementation
docs.text:
Follow these steps in order to build the complete process:

  1. Create BPMN Diagram — Design the end-to-end process flow
  2. Add a Robot — Automate structured task execution
  3. Add an Agent — Add AI-driven decision-making
  4. Add a Human — Configure validation and escalation checkpoints
  5. Add API Calls — Connect to external systems to complete the workflow

Training Environment

Log in at cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs using tenant AgenticPractice.

docs.title: Step 3 — Add an Agent
docs.text: Build a 2-way matching agent and connect it to your Maestro workflow

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Create the 2-Way Matching Agent in Agent Builder, configure its inputs, outputs, and prompts, then connect it to your Maestro process. The agent compares invoices against purchase orders and returns a matching decision.

docs.title: What the Agent Does
docs.text:
The agent receives two JSON documents — an Invoice and a Purchase Order — and:

  1. Compares them line by line, including header, line items, and totals
  2. Identifies discrepancies
  3. Returns a status (Full Match or Failed Match), an HTML comparison table, and a suggested rejection email if needed

docs.title: Part 1: Create the agent
docs.text:
1. In Studio Web, create a new Agent. Name it 2-Way Matching Agent.

  2. When prompted, dismiss Autopilot and select Start fresh.

  3. Configure the Input Arguments using this JSON schema (import via the schema editor):

    {
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["in_PurchaseOrderData", "in_InvoiceData"],
  "properties": {
    "in_InvoiceData": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "JSON object containing Invoice data and line items"
    },
    "in_PurchaseOrderData": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "JSON object containing Purchase Order data and line items"
    }
  }
}

  4. Configure the Output Arguments using this JSON schema:

    {
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["out_Status"],
  "properties": {
    "out_Status": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Status of matching - either 'Full Match' or 'Failed Match'"
    },
    "out_DocumentsHTML": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "HTML code containing side by side comparison"
    },
    "out_SuggestedResponse": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Suggested response to Supplier"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Part 2: Configure prompts
docs.text:
1. Enter the following System Prompt:

    You are accounting automation agent who is performing 2 way matching of external supplier Invoice against Purchase Order issued by our company against each other. Your objective is to detect and list discrepancies between documents, including line item level comparison, which would allow for more accurate record keeping.

  2. Enter the following User Prompt:

    Compare these documents:
Invoice: {{in_InvoiceData}}
Purchase Order: {{in_PurchaseOrderData}}

docs.title: Part 3: Test the agent
docs.text:
1. Run a simple match test using these sample values:

    Invoice: 

    {
  "Header": {
    "CompanyName": "Lion City Tech Pte Ltd",
    "Company Address": "10 Anson Road, #15-01, Singapore 079903",
    "Company Registration Code": "SG202312345N"
  },
  "Lines": [
    {"ID": "1", "Product Name": "Correction Tape", "Unit Price": "0.30", "Quantity": "30"}
  ],
  "Details": {"Subtotal": "9.00", "Tax (GST 9%)": "0.81", "Total": "9.81"}
}

    Provide the matching PO data as the second input. The agent should return Full Match.

  2. Test with a more complex invoice — one with multiple line items where some have slight description variations (e.g., Tape Dispenser appears twice, or a descriptor like (USB-charging) is appended). Note how the agent handles these.

  3. Add the following matching rules to your System Prompt to make the agent's behavior explicit:

    Acceptable variations (count as Full Match): - Identical prices and quantities, with non-exact descriptions - One PO line matching multiple invoice lines if quantities sum correctly - Semantically similar descriptions (e.g., Pen vs Red pen, Phone vs Conference Phone)

    Discrepancies requiring supplier clarification (Failed Match): - Items in the invoice with no matching PO line - Price or quantity differences - Semantically different descriptions - Multiple invoice lines mapping to a single PO line with different prices

docs.title: Part 4: Configure the agent in Maestro
docs.text:
1. Return to your 2-Way Matching Process in Maestro. Select the agent task node in the BPMN canvas.

  2. Set the action to Start and wait for agent. Select 2-Way Matching Agent.

  3. Map the robot outputs to the agent inputs:

    Robot Output Agent Input out_PurchaseOrderData in_PurchaseOrderData out_InvoiceData in_InvoiceData

  4. Add an Exclusive Gateway after the agent task. Connect it to:

    • Payment path (Full Match)
    • Manual review path (Failed Match)

  5. Set the gateway expression for the Full Match path:

    vars.outStatus.ToLower()=="full match"

  6. Run the process in debug mode and confirm the gateway routes correctly based on the agent's decision.

← Step 2: Add a Robot | Next: Add a Human →

docs.title: Step 5 — Add API Calls
docs.text: Send rejection emails and store approved invoices via Integration Service

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Complete the process by connecting two external systems: Gmail (to send rejection emails to suppliers) and Data Fabric (to store approved invoice data for payment processing). Both use UiPath Integration Service, which handles authentication so you don't have to configure credentials manually.

docs.title: Integration Service
docs.text: Integration Service manages authorization and authentication for SaaS platforms. You select a connector and a shared connection, and Maestro handles the rest. No API keys embedded in your process.

docs.title: Part 1: Configure the rejection email
docs.text:
1. In your 2-Way Matching Process in Maestro, select the task on the Reject path.

  2. Set the action type to Execute Connector Activity.

  3. Select the Gmail Connector and then the shared Gmail connection.

  4. Configure the Send Email activity:

    • To: the supplier's email address (from the invoice data)
    • Subject: Invoice rejection notification
    • Body: use out_SuggestedResponse from the agent output — it contains a formatted rejection email with specific discrepancy reasons

  5. Save the task.

docs.title: Part 2: Store approved invoice data in Data Fabric
docs.text:
1. Select the task on the Approve path (after the human approves or after a direct Full Match).

  2. Set the action type to Execute Connector Activity.

  3. Select the Data Fabric connector and configure it to write the approved invoice record to the database. This data becomes available to the finance team for payment processing.

  4. Map the relevant invoice fields as the record payload.

docs.title: Part 3: Test the complete process
docs.text:
1. Run the process several times with different invoice scenarios. After a few runs:

    • Check the Data Fabric database — approved invoices accumulate as records
    • Check the supplier email inbox — rejection emails with formatted discrepancy lists arrive automatically

Your process is complete. It retrieves invoices via robot, matches them using an AI agent, routes exceptions to human reviewers, sends rejection emails via Gmail, and stores approved records in Data Fabric — all orchestrated by Maestro.

← Step 4: Add a Human | Back to Overview

docs.title: Step 4 — Add a Human
docs.text: Import a validation app and configure the human review task

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add a human validation step to the Failed Match path. When the agent flags a mismatch, a reviewer in Action Center sees the invoice and PO side by side and approves or rejects the match. The workflow resumes automatically based on the reviewer's decision.

docs.title: Humans in the Loop
docs.text: Humans are most effective when they have all the information they need on one screen. In this step, you'll import a pre-built validation app that presents the agent's HTML comparison output to the reviewer — no switching between systems, no hunting for data.

docs.title: Part 1: Import the validation app
docs.text:
1. In your workspace, navigate to Apps (or Action Center Apps).

  2. Import the 2WM Validation App Template from the shared templates library.

  3. Review the imported app. It displays:

    • The side-by-side HTML comparison generated by the agent
    • The suggested rejection email
    • Two action buttons: Approve and Reject

docs.title: Part 2: Configure the human validation task
docs.text:
1. In your 2-Way Matching Process in Maestro, select the human task node on the Failed Match path.

  2. Set the action type to Action App Task.

  3. Select the 2WM Validation App you imported.

  4. Set a descriptive task title, for example: Invoice Review Required.

  5. Assign the task to yourself (or a specific user) so it appears in your Action Center queue.

  6. Map the agent outputs to the app inputs:

    Agent Output App Input out_DocumentsHTML Comparison HTML display field out_SuggestedResponse Suggested rejection email field

  7. Save the task configuration.

docs.title: Part 3: Configure the gateway decision logic
docs.text:
1. Add an Exclusive Gateway after the human task. This routes the workflow based on what the reviewer chose.

  2. Configure two outgoing paths:

    • Approve → proceed to payment
    • Reject → send supplier email

  3. Set the expression for the Approve path:

    vars.actionOutput == "Approve"

docs.title: Part 4: Test the human-in-the-loop flow
docs.text:
1. Run the process in debug mode. Trigger a Failed Match scenario so the human task activates.

  2. Open Action Center and find your task. Review the comparison and click Approve or Reject.

  3. Return to the process and confirm it resumed and routed correctly based on your decision.

← Step 3: Add an Agent | Next: Add API Calls →

docs.title: Step 2 — Add a Robot
docs.text: Import your BPMN diagram and connect it to an RPA process

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Create an Agentic Process in Maestro, import the BPMN diagram you built in Step 1, and connect the first workflow task to the Retrieve.Invoice RPA process. By the end of this step, your process will retrieve simulated invoice and PO data and you'll be able to run it end to end in debug mode.

docs.title: Why a Robot Here
docs.text: Hundreds of invoices arrive daily, extracted from ERP and billing systems via RPA — without API access. The Retrieve.Invoice process simulates this retrieval and generates sample Invoice and PO documents as JSON, providing structured input for the agent in the next step.

docs.title: Create the Maestro Agentic Process
docs.text:
1. In Studio Web, create a new Agentic Process.

  2. Import your BPMN diagram. Select the 2-Way Matching Process.bpmn file you exported in Step 1.

  3. Delete the auto-generated empty process placeholder that appears after import.

  4. Rename the process to 2-Way Matching Process.

  5. Rename the project to 2-Way Matching Project.

docs.title: Configure the robot task
docs.text:
1. Select the robot task node in your BPMN canvas.

  2. Open the properties panel and select Start and wait for RPA workflow as the action type.

  3. Search for and select the Retrieve.Invoice process from the 2-Way Matching folder.

  4. Set the in_FailureProbability input argument to 90 (percent). This simulates realistic retrieval conditions during testing.

docs.title: Test the process
docs.text:
1. Click Debug to launch the process.

  2. Wait for the process to complete and review the output. The Retrieve.Invoice process produces JSON with the following structure:

    {
  "Header": {
    "CompanyName": "...",
    "Address": "...",
    "RegistrationCode": "..."
  },
  "Lines": [
    {
      "ID": "1",
      "ProductName": "...",
      "UnitPrice": "...",
      "Quantity": "..."
    }
  ],
  "Details": {
    "Subtotal": "...",
    "Tax": "...",
    "Total": "..."
  }
}

  3. Confirm that both invoice and PO data were generated in the output.

The robot is now connected and returning structured data. In the next step, you'll add an agent that compares the two documents.

← Step 1: Create BPMN Diagram | Next: Add an Agent →

docs.title: Step 1 — Create BPMN Diagram
docs.text: Model the invoice matching process before building it

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Design the invoice matching workflow as a BPMN diagram. This diagram becomes the blueprint you'll import into Maestro in the next step.

docs.title: The Process You're Modeling
docs.text:
A supplier sends an invoice containing a header, line items, and footer. Your automation compares this invoice against the associated Purchase Order (PO).

Three outcomes are possible:

Outcome Next Action Full match Update UiPath Data Service and proceed to payment Partial match / mismatch Route to Accounting Team for manual review No match / rejected Send notification email to supplier

docs.title: BPMN Diagramming Tool
docs.text: Build your diagram at bpmn.uipath.com. When finished, export it as a .bpmn file — you'll import it into Maestro in Step 2.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open bpmn.uipath.com in your browser.

  2. Create a new diagram and model the invoice matching process:

    • A Start Event triggers when a new invoice arrives
    • A Robot task retrieves the invoice and associated PO data
    • An Agent task compares invoice against PO
    • An Exclusive Gateway routes based on the comparison result:
      • Full match → update Data Service → End
      • Mismatch → Accounting Team review → End
      • Rejected → send supplier notification email → End

  3. Review the process flow to make sure all paths have an End Event.

  4. Export the diagram as a .bpmn file. Save it as 2-Way Matching Process.bpmn.

You now have a complete process blueprint ready to import into Maestro.

← Back to Overview | Next: Add a Robot →

docs.title: Invoice Matching with IXP
docs.text: 2-Way matching with Intelligent eXtraction & Processing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This exercise builds on the standard invoice matching process by adding IXP (Intelligent eXtraction & Processing) for automated data extraction from PDF invoices. IXP handles unstructured documents and provides confidence scores that drive automated escalation decisions.

docs.title: What IXP Adds
docs.text: Capability Description PDF extraction Reads and extracts data from unstructured invoice PDFs Confidence scoring Each extracted field gets a confidence score Automated escalation Low-confidence results automatically route to human review

docs.title: How It Fits the Process
docs.text:
The full workflow:

Robot retrieves PDFs
  → Agent extracts data using IXP
      → High confidence: Agent queries ERP for PO data
          → Agent validates and decides (match/reject)
      → Low confidence: Escalate to human for review
  → API calls complete the process

docs.title: Five-Step Implementation
docs.text:
1. Create BPMN Process — Design the IXP-enhanced workflow
  2. Configure a Robot — Set up invoice PDF retrieval
  3. Configure an Agent — Extract data, query ERP, validate, decide
  4. Configure Human Validation — Handle low-confidence escalations
  5. Configure API Integration — Connect to external systems

Training Environment

Log in at cloud.uipath.com/tpenlabs using tenant AgenticPractice.

docs.title: Step 3 — Configure an Agent
docs.text: Extract invoice data using IXP, look up the PO, and perform matching

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Configure the 2-Way Matching Agent with two tools: an IXP extraction tool and an RPA workflow tool that queries the Purchase Order database. The agent receives a PDF invoice, extracts structured data from it, looks up the corresponding PO, and returns a matching decision.

docs.title: How the Agent Works
docs.text:
The agent follows this sequence:

  1. Receives the invoice PDF filename
  2. Calls the IXP tool to extract structured invoice data (header, line items, totals, PO ID)
  3. Uses the extracted PO ID to call the RPA workflow tool and retrieve the matching Purchase Order from the database
  4. Compares invoice against PO and returns: Full Match or Failed Match

docs.title: PO ID Format
docs.text: PO IDs in this exercise follow the format PO- followed by digits — for example, PO-123456. The agent uses this format when filtering the database query.

docs.title: Part 1: Create the agent
docs.text:
1. In Studio Web, create a new Agent. Name it 2-Way Matching Agent.

  2. Configure the Input Argument:

    Argument Type Description InvoicePDF File The PDF invoice file retrieved from Storage Bucket

  3. Configure the Output Arguments:

    Argument Type Description out_Status String Full Match or Failed Match out_DocumentsHTML String Side-by-side HTML comparison out_SuggestedResponse String Suggested rejection email to supplier out_POID String The PO ID extracted from the invoice

  4. Save the argument configuration.

docs.title: Part 2: Configure the prompts
docs.text:
1. Enter the following User Prompt:

    Analyze {{InvoicePDF}}

  2. Configure the System Prompt with matching rules and tool instructions. Include instructions for calling the IXP tool first, extracting the PO ID, then calling the PO lookup tool.

docs.title: Part 3: Add the IXP tool
docs.text:
1. Go to the Tools tab and add the IXP tool.

  2. Select the InvoicesIXP project as the IXP extraction source.

  3. Configure the tool description:

    Use this tool to extract structured data from the invoice PDF, including header information, line items, totals, and the Purchase Order ID.

  4. Save the IXP tool configuration.

docs.title: Part 4: Add the PO lookup tool
docs.text:
1. Add a second tool: an RPA Workflow tool.

  2. Select Query Entity Records from the PurchaseOrdersDatabase source.

  3. Configure the filter: POID equals in_POID.

  4. Configure the tool description:

    Use this tool to retrieve Purchase Order data from the database. Filter by POID using the PO ID extracted from the invoice. PO IDs start with "PO-" followed by digits.

  5. Save the PO lookup tool configuration.

docs.title: Part 5: Test the agent
docs.text:
1. Test the agent with a sample PDF invoice from the Storage Bucket. Confirm the agent calls the IXP tool to extract data.

  2. Review the IXP extraction results — verify the agent identified the PO ID correctly.

  3. Confirm the agent called the PO lookup tool with the extracted PO ID.

  4. Review the final agent output — verify out_Status, out_DocumentsHTML, out_SuggestedResponse, and out_POID are all populated.

docs.title: Part 6: Connect the agent to Maestro
docs.text:
1. Return to your 2-Way Matching IXP Process in Maestro. Select the agent task node.

  2. Set the action to Start and wait for agent. Select 2-Way Matching Agent.

  3. Map the robot output to the agent input:

    Robot Output Agent Input out_FileName InvoicePDF

  4. Add an Exclusive Gateway after the agent task. Configure the expression for the Full Match path:

    vars.outStatus.ToLower()=="full match"

  5. Run the process in debug mode and confirm the gateway routes correctly.

← Step 2: Configure a Robot | Next: Configure Human Validation →

docs.title: Step 5 — Configure API Integration
docs.text: Connect Gmail and Data Fabric to complete the IXP process

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add the same two API integrations as the standard exercise: Gmail for rejection emails and Data Fabric for storing approved invoice records. Both use Integration Service connectors already configured in your tenant.

docs.title: Part 1: Configure the rejection email
docs.text:
1. In your 2-Way Matching IXP Process in Maestro, select the task on the Reject path.

  2. Set the action type to Execute Connector Activity.

  3. Select the Gmail Connector and the shared Gmail connection.

  4. Configure the Send Email activity:

    • To: the supplier's email address from the extracted invoice data
    • Body: use out_SuggestedResponse — the agent's formatted rejection email with specific discrepancy reasons

  5. Save the task.

docs.title: Part 2: Store approved invoice data in Data Fabric
docs.text:
1. Select the task on the Approve path.

  2. Set the action type to Execute Connector Activity.

  3. Select the Data Fabric connector and configure it to write the approved invoice record for payment processing.

docs.title: Part 3: Test the complete process
docs.text:
1. Run the process several times. After a few runs:

    • Check Data Fabric — approved invoices accumulate as records
    • Check the supplier inbox — formatted rejection emails arrive automatically

Your IXP-enhanced process is complete. It retrieves invoice PDFs via robot, extracts and validates data using IXP, queries the PO database, routes exceptions to human review, sends rejection emails, and stores approved records — all orchestrated by Maestro.

← Step 4: Configure Human Validation | Back to Overview

docs.title: Step 4 — Configure Human Validation
docs.text: Import the IXP validation app and configure the human review task

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Add a human validation step on the Failed Match path. When the agent flags a mismatch, a reviewer in Action Center sees the comparison and approves or rejects it. The workflow resumes based on their decision.

docs.title: The IXP Validation App
docs.text:
This exercise uses a different template than the standard exercise:

Exercise App Template Standard (JSON) 2WM Validation App Template IXP (PDF) 2WM Validation App IXP Template

The IXP template is adapted to work with data extracted from PDFs via IXP.

docs.title: Part 1: Import the validation app
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Apps in your workspace.

  2. Import the 2WM Validation App IXP Template from the shared templates library.

  3. Review the imported app. It displays the agent's HTML comparison output and provides Approve and Reject buttons.

docs.title: Part 2: Configure the human task in Maestro
docs.text:
1. In your 2-Way Matching IXP Process, select the human task node on the Failed Match path.

  2. Set the action type to Action App Task.

  3. Select the 2WM Validation App IXP you imported.

  4. Set the task title, for example: Invoice Review Required.

  5. Assign the task to yourself.

  6. Map the agent outputs to the app inputs:

    Agent Output App Input out_DocumentsHTML Comparison display field out_SuggestedResponse Suggested rejection email field

  7. Save the task configuration.

docs.title: Part 3: Configure the gateway decision logic
docs.text:
1. Add an Exclusive Gateway after the human task.

  2. Set Reject as the default path (it will be taken if the expression on the Approve path is not met).

  3. Configure the expression for the Approve path using the Expression Editor:

    vars.actionOutput == "Approve"

  4. Connect the Approve path to the payment task and the Reject path to the supplier notification task.

docs.title: Part 4: Test the human validation flow
docs.text:
1. Run the process in debug mode. Trigger a Failed Match so the human task activates.

  2. Open Action Center and find your task. Review the comparison and click Approve or Reject.

  3. Confirm the process resumed and routed correctly.

  4. Review the execution trace in Maestro to verify the correct path was taken.

  5. Run additional test scenarios — one Approve and one Reject — to confirm both paths work.

  6. Review the end-to-end process flow animation.

← Step 3: Configure an Agent | Next: Configure API Integration →

docs.title: Step 2 — Configure a Robot
docs.text: Set up invoice PDF retrieval from a Storage Bucket

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Create the Maestro Agentic Process, import your BPMN diagram, and connect the first task to the RetrieveInvoiceDocument RPA process. Unlike the standard exercise where the robot returns JSON, this robot retrieves a PDF file name from a Storage Bucket — the agent will extract structured data from the PDF in the next step.

docs.title: Key Difference from the Standard Exercise
docs.text: Standard Exercise IXP Exercise Robot process Retrieve.Invoice RetrieveInvoiceDocument Robot folder 2-Way Matching 2-Way Matching IXP Output Invoice + PO as JSON PDF filename in Storage Bucket

docs.title: Create the Maestro Agentic Process
docs.text:
1. In Studio Web, create a new Agentic Process.

  2. Import your BPMN diagram. Select the 2-Way Matching IXP Process.bpmn file you exported in Step 1.

  3. Delete the auto-generated empty process placeholder.

  4. Rename the process to 2-Way Matching IXP Process.

  5. Rename the project to 2-Way Matching IXP Project.

docs.title: Configure the robot task
docs.text:
1. Select the robot task node in the BPMN canvas.

  2. Open the properties panel and set the action to Start and wait for RPA workflow.

  3. Search for and select the RetrieveInvoiceDocument process from the 2-Way Matching IXP folder.

  4. Review the process outputs. The robot returns a PDF filename stored in a Storage Bucket — this is what the agent will use to access and process the invoice document.

docs.title: Test the process
docs.text:
1. Click Debug to launch the process.

  2. Confirm the robot completed successfully and returned a PDF filename in the output variables.

  3. Review the output variable mapping to verify the filename is accessible for the next step.

  4. Close debug mode and save the process.

The robot is now retrieving invoices as PDFs. In the next step, you'll configure the agent to extract structured data from those PDFs using IXP.

← Step 1: Create BPMN Process | Next: Configure an Agent →

docs.title: Step 1 — Create BPMN Process
docs.text: Model the IXP-enhanced invoice matching workflow

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Design the BPMN diagram for the IXP-based invoice matching process. The process is structurally similar to the standard version, with one key difference: the robot retrieves a PDF document (not pre-structured JSON), and the agent extracts structured data from it using IXP (Intelligent eXtraction & Processing).

docs.title: Matching Variants
docs.text:
This exercise uses 2-way matching — comparing an invoice against a Purchase Order. For reference, more advanced variants exist:

Variant Documents Compared 2-way Invoice + Purchase Order 3-way Invoice + Purchase Order + Goods Receipt Note 4-way Invoice + Purchase Order + Goods Receipt Note + Inspection Report

You're building the 2-way variant.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open bpmn.uipath.com in your browser.

  2. Create a new diagram and model the invoice matching process:

    • A Start Event triggers when a new invoice PDF arrives
    • A Robot task retrieves the PDF from a Storage Bucket
    • An Agent task extracts data using IXP, looks up the PO, and performs matching
    • An Exclusive Gateway routes based on the agent's decision:
      • Full match → update Data Service → End
      • Mismatch → Human review → End
      • Rejected → Send supplier notification → End

  3. Review all paths and confirm each has an End Event.

  4. Export the diagram as a .bpmn file. Save it as 2-Way Matching IXP Process.bpmn.

← Back to Overview | Next: Configure a Robot →
